feedback.text: I am writing as a concerned consumer and EU citizen to urge you to include clear protections in the forthcoming Digital Fairness Act. Recent actions by several digital platforms, removing paid products, geo-blocking content, and degrading services without warning, pose a direct threat to our fundamental right to access and enjoy lawfully purchased digital goods. Over the past year, users across the EU have witnessed games suddenly delisted from online stores, leaving paid DLC and in-game purchases inaccessible. Likewise, financial data providers have imposed abrupt regional restrictions, denying subscribers information they have already paid for. These practices erode consumer trust, fragment the internal market, and grant platforms unchecked power to decide which content remains available. A personal example: Im an avid horror-game fan and paid full price for the indie title Mouthwashing, a low-poly, psychologically gripping experience that went viral for its innovative narrative. Yet in July 2025 it quietly vanished from Itch.ios search results and was de-indexed in October 2024 solely over a technicality with its Steam link. Although still purchasable via a direct Steam link, its removal from the platforms search makes it virtually invisible to new players, undermining consumer choice and transparency . Such conduct runs counter to Article 17 of the Digital Services Act, which guarantees transparency and fairness in platform moderation, and clashes with the EU Charter of Fundamental Rightsparticularly property rights (Article 17) and freedom to conduct a business (Article 16). Without robust enforcement, dominant platforms will continue to erode consumer rights and stifle competition. These anti-consumer practices threaten far more than the world of gaming. If platforms are free to block or disable content without warning, entire sectors, from online education and digital health services to financial platforms and cloud-productivity tools, could face sudden shutdowns of paid features, leaving users stranded and businesses unable to deliver critical services. Such unchecked power undermines the integrity of the digital single market and risks chilling innovation, investment, and consumer confidence across the EU. I urge the Commission to act decisively to prevent this dangerous precedent. By establishing clear rules and enforcement rights now, you will safeguard not only gamers but every European who relies on digital services for work, learning, and daily life.
